title: Site Reliability Engineering — Staff+ FAANG Interview Prep
description: Comprehensive deep-dive into Site Reliability Engineering at Staff+ FAANG engineering level. Covers SRE foundations (SLIs, SLOs, error budgets), monitoring & observability (golden signals, metrics/traces/logs), alerting & on-call (fatigue reduction, escalation), incident management (severity levels, postmortems), capacity planning (forecasting, load testing), change management (canary, rollback, feature flags), toil automation, distributed tracing & debugging, disaster recovery (RTO/RPO, backup, failover), data-driven SRE (burn-rate alerting, dashboards), reliability in the SDLC (PRR, testing for reliability), SRE culture & team structure (FAANG models), global-scale reliability (multi-region, DNS, edge), IaC & tooling (Terraform, GitOps), cost-aware SRE (efficiency, waste reduction, right-sizing), and Staff+ interview strategy — every topic a Staff+ SRE engineer must own.
details:
Staff+ FAANG Interview Strategy for SRE: SRE interviews at Staff+ level test (1) System design for reliability — design systems that survive single-region outages, data corruption, and cascading failures, (2) Incident command — how you lead a major outage response, structure communication, and drive to mitigation, (3) Data-driven operations — SLOs, error budgets, burn-rate alerting, efficiency metrics, (4) Automation mindset — measuring toil, building automation, designing self-healing systems, (5) Cultural influence — building reliability culture, blameless postmortems, error-budget buy-in from product teams.

Company emphasis: Google: SLO/error budget theology, Borg reliability, Dapper tracing. Amazon: Design for failure (everything fails all the time), COE (Correction of Error) process, cell-based architecture. Meta: Scaled agile SRE, automation-first culture, TAO caching reliability. Netflix: Chaos engineering (Chaos Monkey, Kong), developer-owned ops, CDN reliability (Open Connect).

Key principle: The best Staff+ SRE answers reveal experiential knowledge of production reality — not just textbook patterns, but what actually happens at 3 AM during a SEV-1 when your monitoring is down and the database is corrupted and the person who knows the system is on vacation. The Staff+ SRE has seen this before and has the battle scars to prove it.

sections.title: 1. SRE Foundations: SLIs, SLOs & Error Budgets
sections.description:
SRE · SLO · ERROR BUDGET · FOUNDATIONS

Staff+ expectation: SRE is what happens when you treat operations as a software engineering problem. At Staff+ level you don't just define SLOs — you design error budget policies, make build-vs-run tradeoffs, and align reliability with business goals.

SLI (Service Level Indicator): A carefully defined quantitative measure of some aspect of service level. Examples: request latency (p99 < 200ms), availability (fraction of successful requests), throughput (requests/sec), durability (fraction of writes preserved). Key property: SLIs must be measurable in production.

SLO (Service Level Objective): Target value or range for an SLI over a time window: "99.9% of requests complete in <200ms over 30 days." SLOs are the contract between the SRE team and the product team.

Error Budget: The acceptable amount of unreliability = 100% – SLO. A 99.9% SLO gives a 0.1% error budget (43m 12s of downtime/month). Error budget policy: if budget is consumed, deployments are frozen or rolled back. This resolves the inherent tension between velocity and reliability.

Staff+ insight on SLO math: Error budget burn rate = actual error rate / allowed error rate. Burn rate > 1 means consuming budget faster than planned. Multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting (e.g., 2h at 14.4x + 6h at 6x + 3d at 1x) catches fast and slow burns without noisy pages.

99.9%
Typical production SLO (43 min downtime/month)
70%
Of error budget consumption at Google triggers deployment freeze
14.4x
Fast burn rate alert threshold (2h window)

sections.title: 2. Monitoring & Observability: Metrics, Traces, Logs
sections.description:
OBSERVABILITY · GOLDEN SIGNALS · TELEMETRY

Staff+ expectation: Observability is not just dashboards — it’s the ability to understand any system state without shipping new code. You must design telemetry pipelines at scale (100K+ events/s), choose cardinality boundaries, and build meaningful dashboards that reduce MTTR.

The Four Golden Signals (Google SRE Book): (1) Latency — time to service a request (distinguish success vs error latency). (2) Traffic — demand on the system (QPS, concurrent users, throughput). (3) Errors — rate of failed requests (explicit 5xx, implicit 200s with wrong results, slow responses). (4) Saturation — how "full" the service is (CPU, memory, disk, connections, quota exhaustion).

USE Method (Brendan Gregg): Utilization, Saturation, Errors for every resource. For CPU: utilization = % busy, saturation = run-queue length, errors = ECC memory errors or scheduler throttling.

RED Method (Tom Wilkie): Rate, Errors, Duration for every service. Aligned with the four golden signals but simpler to implement per-service.

Three Pillars:

  • Metrics (Prometheus, M3): Numeric time-series. Low cardinality (10-50 labels). Retention: hours at raw, months at aggregated. Storage: TSDB with 2-16 bytes per sample.
  • Logs (ELK, Loki, Bigtable): Event records. High volume (TB/day). Structured (JSON) vs unstructured. Sampling at high volume.
  • Traces (Jaeger, Zipkin, Honeycomb): Request lifecycle across services. Sampling (head-based 1-10%, tail-based for slow/error). Context propagation (W3C tracecontext, baggage).
LatencyTrafficErrorsSaturation
SignalMetrics exampleAlert thresholdDetection window
p99 < 200ms> 500ms for 5minMoving window
QPS, concurrent conns> 2x baseline for 1hDaily seasonality
HTTP 5xx rate> 0.1% for 5min1-5 min windows
CPU%, queue depth> 80% for 10minHourly avg

sections.title: 3. Alerting & On-Call: Reduce Fatigue, Improve Signal
sections.description:
ALERTING · ON-CALL · PAGER DUTY · ESCALATION

Staff+ expectation: Alert fatigue destroys SRE teams. Design alerting that pages only when human action is required. Automate everything else. Understand escalation, rotation (follow-the-sun vs primary+secondary), and incident command structure.

Alert Types: Page (immediate human action required), Ticket (action required but not urgent, e.g., within 24h), Log (low-priority, processed during business hours). At Google, <5% of alerts should be pages. If >5% are noisy, fix the alert or the system.

Alert Design Patterns:

  • Dead Man Switch: Cron that must succeed or it pages. Example: the cron itself could die, so a watchdog must detect it.
  • Multi-condition: Page only when both A and B are true (e.g., high latency AND error rate elevated — not just one).
  • Rate-based: Page on error rate increase, not error count. A batch job may have 1M requests; 100 errors is fine. A critical service may see 1000 req/s; 10 errors is an emergency.
  • Absence of data: If you haven’t heard from a service for N minutes, page. This catches silent failures.
Page thresholdEscalationRotationHandoff
PracticeGoodBadRule of thumb
>1% error rate >5minAny 5xxP50 request = 1 page/week/engineer
Level 1: 5min, L2: 15min, L3: 30minPage everyoneAuto-escalate every 5-10 min
8-12h shifts, weekly rotation24/7 same personMax 12h shifts to avoid burnout
Shadowed overlap, written summaryOne-line Slack msg15-min structured handoff

On-Call Culture: Every engineer on-call experiences the pain of their own systems. This forces reliability improvements. At Google, SRE teams spend 50% of time on operational work (including on-call) and 50% on engineering. If ops exceeds 50%, the team must automate or reduce toil. At Netflix, the developer who wrote the code is on-call for it.

sections.title: 4. Incident Management: Command, Control & Communication
sections.description:
INCIDENT · RESPONSE · POSTMORTEM · BLAMELESS

Staff+ expectation: When things break (and they will), you need a practiced incident response process. At Staff+ level, you may be the Incident Commander (IC) for a major outage. Understand the command structure, communication channels, and the postmortem process.

Incident Command System (ICS): IC coordinates overall response. Operations Lead executes mitigation. Communications Lead manages stakeholder updates. Scribe documents timeline. SMEs (Subject Matter Experts) investigate specific subsystems.

Incident Severity Levels (Google):

  • SEV-1: Critical user-facing impact. Active data loss. Page IC immediately. < ~5m response target.
  • SEV-2: Major feature degraded or partial outage. Page within 15m. Fix within business hours or 24h.
  • SEV-3: Minor feature issue. Investigate next business day.
  • SEV-4: Bug, cosmetic, docs. Normal backlog.

Postmortem Culture: Blameless, written within 72h, action items tracked. SRE teams at Google average 1 postmortem per 2-3 major deployments. Rule: Every SEV-1 or repeated SEV-2 requires a postmortem.

Postmortem Structure: Summary → Timeline (with timestamps) → Impact (users affected, $ cost) → Root cause (technical, not human) → Trigger → Contributing factors → Detection (how was it found? could it be faster?) → Resolution → Action items with owners.

<5 min
SEV-1 response target
72h
Postmortem deadline
~40%
Of SEV-1s at Google involve change management

sections.title: 5. Capacity Planning: Forecasting, Modeling & Right-Sizing
sections.description:
CAPACITY · FORECASTING · RIGHT-SIZING · DEMAND

Staff+ expectation: Capacity planning is the art of predicting resource needs before they become crises. At Staff+, you own the supply-demand model, run load tests, and negotiate resource commitments with product teams.

Demand Forecasting: Use historical growth curves (linear, exponential, seasonal ARIMA). Key inputs: user growth rate (product roadmap), feature adoption projections, pricing changes, marketing campaigns. At Meta, capacity planners meet with PMs quarterly to review product roadmap impact on infrastructure.

Supply Planning: Lead times: on-demand cloud = seconds, reserved instances = hours, colo rack deployment = 4-8 weeks, new data center = 12-24 months. At Staff+ level, you plan 12-24 months ahead for hardware procurement while using cloud elasticity for short-term spikes.

ShortMediumLong
TermHorizonApproachRisk
0-3 monthsAuto-scaling, cloud burstCost overrun
3-12 monthsReserved instances, capacity reservationsOver-provisioning
12-24 monthsHardware procurement, DC buildUnder-estimation

Load Testing: Types: smoke (single user), stress (beyond peak), endurance (sustained over hours), spike (sudden burst). Tools: Locust, k6, vegeta, internal frameworks (Google’s F1 load tester, Meta’s Treadmill). Staff+ must design load tests that mimic realistic traffic patterns, not uniform request rates.

Right-Sizing: Over-provisioning = waste, under-provisioning = outages. At Google, clusters run at 40-50% CPU utilization on average (buffer for spikes and headroom). Borg’s resource estimation from historical usage achieves 20-50% better packing than request-based scheduling.

sections.title: 6. Change Management: Deployments, Canary & Rollback
sections.description:
CHANGE · DEPLOYMENT · CANARY · ROLLBACK

Staff+ expectation: Change is the #1 cause of production incidents at FAANG. Staff+ engineers design deployment pipelines that minimize blast radius, automate rollbacks, and make deploys boring.

Progressive Delivery: Deploy to a small subset before full rollout. Stages: (1) Build + unit/integration tests, (2) Canary (1-5% of traffic, watch metrics for 5-30 min), (3) Rolling update (10-25-50-100% over minutes), (4) Full rollout. At each stage, automated health checks gate progression.

Canary Analysis: Compare error rate, latency, and saturation between canary and baseline. Use statistical tests (Mann-Whitney U, Kolmogorov-Smirnov) to detect regressions. If difference exceeds threshold (e.g., error rate increase >0.1% with p<0.05), auto-rollback. Netflix: automated canary analysis for every deployment.

Rollback Strategies: Revert (git revert + redeploy, fast but may leave state changes), Feature flag (instant toggle-off, no deploy needed), Blue/green (flip traffic from new to old version, instant). Feature flags are the recommended approach for Staff+ level systems: you can disable a problematic change without a full redeploy.

#1
Source of FAANG production incidents: changes
1-5%
Typical canary traffic percentage
<30 min
Canary observation window

sections.title: 7. Toil Automation: Eliminate Operational Drudgery
sections.description:
TOIL · AUTOMATION · TOIL REDUCTION · MANUAL OPS

Staff+ expectation: Toil is manual, repetitive, automatable work with no enduring value. At Staff+, you measure toil, set reduction targets, and build automation that frees your team for engineering. Google SRE teams spend max 50% of time on toil; the rest on project work.

What Is Toil?: (1) Manual (requires human hands), (2) Repetitive (same steps each time), (3) Automatable (no human judgment needed), (4) Tactical (interrupt-driven, not strategic), (5) No enduring value (fixes a symptom once, not the root cause).

Toil Examples: Restarting crashed processes, manually scaling servers, rotating credentials by hand, triaging the same alert daily (fix the root cause instead), updating configs across 100 servers, granting access to a system.

Toil Reduction Framework:

  1. Measure: Track toil hours per week via self-reporting or ticket classification.
  2. Set targets: Reduce toil by 20% per quarter. If a team spends >50% on toil, freeze feature work.
  3. Automate: Each repetitive task gets a runbook or full automation. Target: automate 80% of repetitive tasks.
  4. Eliminate: Is the task necessary at all? Can the system be changed so the task disappears?
L1 ManualL2 DocumentedL3 ScriptedL4 AutomatedL5 Eliminated
LevelDefinitionToil %Example
No automation, tribal knowledge>50%Manual deploy via SSH
Runbooks exist, still manual30-50%Wiki page for restarting
One-off scripts, engineer runs15-30%restart.sh script
System handles without human5-15%Auto-restart on failure
Problem doesn’t exist<5%Auto-healing, no restart needed

sections.title: 8. Distributed Tracing & Production Debugging
sections.description:
TRACING · DEBUGGING · CONTINUOUS PROFILING · DAPPER

Staff+ expectation: Debugging distributed systems is the hardest skill to learn. At Staff+ level, you use distributed tracing, continuous profiling, and structured debugging to find needle-in-a-haystack problems across 100+ services.

Distributed Tracing (Dapper/Google 2010): Each request gets a trace ID. Spans represent work done by a single service. Parent-child span hierarchy. Metadata: service name, operation, start time, duration, status, tags. Sampling is essential: head-based (1/1024, always sample slow ones) or tail-based (only sample interesting traces like errors or p99+ latencies).

Context Propagation: W3C TraceContext (traceparent header: 00-traceid-spanid-flags) and baggage (tracestate for vendor-specific data). Every RPC framework must propagate context automatically (gRPC middleware, HTTP interceptors).

Continuous Profiling (Google, Meta): Sample CPU, memory, IO (perf, eBPF) at 10-100 Hz across all production hosts. Stored in centralized profiling DB. Use to find: CPU hot spots, memory leaks, lock contention, allocation-heavy paths. Tools: Google’s profiler (built into Borg), Pyroscope, Parca.

<100µs
Overhead per traced request
1/1024
Default Google tracing sample rate
100 Hz
Continuous profiling sample rate

sections.title: 9. Disaster Recovery: RTO/RPO, Backup & Failover
sections.description:
DISASTER RECOVERY · RTO · RPO · BACKUP · FAILOVER

Staff+ expectation: Disaster recovery (DR) is not just backups — it’s designing for inevitable region failures, data corruption, and operator errors. At FAANG, DR is tested quarterly with scheduled failover exercises.

Key Metrics:

  • RTO (Recovery Time Objective): Maximum acceptable time to restore service. Financial systems: minutes. Internal tools: hours. Consumer apps: minutes.
  • RPO (Recovery Point Objective): Maximum acceptable data loss. Banking: zero (RPO=0 through synchronous replication). Analytics: hours (RPO=1h through async replication).

DR Strategies (in order of increasing RTO/RPO):

  1. Active-Active (multi-region): All regions serve traffic. RTO < 1min, RPO near 0. Very expensive. Used by Google, Amazon, Meta for critical services.
  2. Active-Passive (warm standby): Primary active, standby ready. RTO = minutes, RPO = seconds (async replication). Used for 80% of enterprise systems.
  3. Pilot Light: Core data replicated, compute scaled up on failover. RTO = tens of minutes, RPO = minutes.
  4. Backup & Restore: Cold DR. RTO = hours, RPO = hours (last backup). Cheapest but longest recovery.
Active-ActiveActive-PassivePilot LightBackup/Restore
StrategyRTORPOCostFAANG use
< 1 min~05-10xSpanner, DynamoDB
1-15 min< 30s2-3xMost internal services
15-60 min< 1h1.5-2xDev/test instances
1-24+ h1-24 h1xArchival data

Backup Design: Full (slow, weekly), Incremental (fast, daily), Differential (faster than full, captures changes since last full). 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 different media, 1 off-site. At FAANG scale: backup to different region or availability zone. Test restore monthly without telling engineers when the test will happen.

Failover Testing: Game days, Chaos Engineering (Chaos Monkey, AWS Fault Injection Simulator). Start with single-AZ failure, progress to full-region failover. At Netflix: Chaos Kong (simulates AWS region failure). At Google: DiRT (Disaster Recovery Testing) runs quarterly with 90% of services required to participate.

sections.title: 10. Data-Driven SRE: Dashboards, Analytics & Burn Rate
sections.description:
DASHBOARDS · BURN RATE · ANALYTICS · DATA-DRIVEN

Staff+ expectation: At Staff+ level, you don’t just build dashboards — you build decision-support tools. Error budget burn rates drive deployment freezes. SLO dashboards drive capacity planning. Data-driven SRE means every ops decision has a metric behind it.

Dashboard Design Principles: (1) One screen, no scrolling. (2) Start with the SLO compliance graph (the "North Star"). (3) Show burn rate, error rate, latency. (4) Link to runbooks. (5) Target audience: on-call engineer for first 30 seconds. Google: every critical service has a "Service Dashboard" that the on-call engineer checks first.

Multi-Window, Multi-Burn-Rate Alerting (Google): The standard approach to avoid both alert fatigue and missed outages. Three alert windows per SLO:

  • Fast burn (critical, page): 1% budget consumed in < 1h → burn rate 14.4x. Check every 2h.
  • Medium burn (warning, page): 5% budget consumed in < 6h → burn rate 6x. Check every 30min.
  • Slow burn (ticket): 10% budget consumed in < 3d → burn rate 1x. Check every 5min.

Staff+ insight: This avoids the "Friday afternoon gradual degradation" problem: a 1% error rate increase that’s invisible in the 30-day SLO but will eat the entire error budget over the weekend.

< 1h
Fast burn detection (14.4x rate)
3 d
Slow burn detection (1x rate)
100%
Dashboard should show SLO compliance

sections.title: 11. Reliability in the SDLC: Design for Production
sections.description:
SDLC · DESIGN REVIEW · TESTING · PRODUCTION READINESS

Staff+ expectation: Reliability must be designed in from day one, not bolted on after an outage. At Staff+, you participate in design reviews with an SRE lens: "What happens when this service fails?"

Production Readiness Review (PRR): Before a service can serve production traffic, it must pass a PRR. Common checklists at FAANG:

  • Monitoring: SLIs defined and measured. Dashboards exist. Alerts configured.
  • On-call: Runbooks exist. Team is trained. Escalation defined.
  • Capacity: Load test completed. Auto-scaling configured. Quota limits set.
  • Dependencies: External dependencies have SLAs. Degradation behavior is defined.
  • Deployment: Canary stages defined. Rollback tested. Feature flags for new features.
  • Disaster recovery: RTO/RPO defined. Backup verified. Failover tested.
DesignDevelopPre-prodProdOperations
PhaseSRE activityKey questionGate
Threat modeling, SLO definitionWhat are the failure modes?SLO review
Testing strategy, error injectionDoes the system handle faults?Test coverage gate
PRR, load test, security scanIs it production-ready?PRR sign-off
Deploy canary, monitor burn rateIs it safe to proceed?Canary health check
Incident response, postmortemWhat did we learn?Action items tracked

Error Budget-Driven Development: If error budget is exhausted, feature development is frozen until reliability is restored. This aligns incentives: product teams must invest in reliability (or accept a lower SLO). At Google, this is a hard rule enforced by SRE.

Testing for Reliability: Unit tests (90%+ coverage for critical paths), Integration tests (service contracts via Pact or similar), End-to-end (smoke tests for critical user journeys), Load tests (sustained at 2x peak traffic), Fault injection (kill a random instance, block a dependency port).

sections.title: 12. SRE Culture & Team Structure at FAANG
sections.description:
SRE CULTURE · TEAM STRUCTURE · SRE VS DEVOPS

Staff+ expectation: SRE is as much a cultural movement as a technical practice. At Staff+ level, you shape the reliability culture across the org. You establish SLO culture, blameless postmortem norms, and operational excellence standards.

SRE vs DevOps: Same goal (reliability + velocity), different approach. DevOps: culture and practices for everyone. SRE: specific implementation of DevOps principles with error budgets and toil caps. Google’s SRE model is the most codified: SRE teams own production, set SLOs, enforce error budgets, and cap toil at 50%.

SRE Team Topology:

  • Embedded SRE: SREs embedded in product teams. Share on-call. Best for high-criticality services.
  • Consulting SRE: Central SRE team advises product teams. Product teams own on-call. Common at Amazon.
  • Platform SRE: Central team builds reliability platforms (deployment, monitoring, chaos). Product teams self-serve. Common at Netflix and Meta.
  • Hybrid: Embedded SREs for critical services + consulting/platform for the rest. Common at Google.
ModelOn-callToil capError budgetPostmortems
DimensionGoogleAmazonMetaNetflix
Embedded+PlatformConsultingPlatformPlatform
Product+SREProductProductProduct dev
50%No capNo capVaries
Hard enforcementSoft guidanceSoft guidanceHard for infra
Blameless, trackedCOE, action itemsBlameless, internalBlameless, blog

Staff+ influence pattern: You don't mandate reliability from above — you make it visible. Show teams their error budget. Let them feel the pain of on-call for their own systems. Celebrate teams with high reliability. The best SRE culture is one where engineers want to be on-call because they care about their service.

sections.title: 13. Global-Scale Reliability: Multi-Region, Edge & DNS
sections.description:
GLOBAL · MULTI-REGION · DNS · EDGE · GSLB

Staff+ expectation: Global services must survive region outages, DNS propagation delays, and edge cache failures. At Staff+, you design the global traffic management architecture and run region-failover exercises.

DNS-Based Global Load Balancing (GSLB): Returns different IPs based on geo/health/latency. TTL determines how fast failover happens. 30s TTL = 30s to shift traffic (plus client DNS caching). 300s = slower but fewer DNS query storms. Health checks: every 5-10s, if N consecutive failures, mark unhealthy. Fastest: 30-60s detection.

Anycast: Same IP announced from multiple locations. BGP routes to nearest. Faster failover (BGP convergence 30-120s) but complex. Used for DNS, CDN, DDoS scrubbing. Caveat: BGP convergence is not instantaneous — 30-60s for intra-AS, 60-120s for inter-AS.

Edge Caching for Reliability: CDN (CloudFront, Cloudflare, Fastly) absorbs traffic during origin failures. Stale-while-revalidate: serve stale content while fetching fresh. 604 status (no content) detection triggers fallback to stale cache. At Cloudflare: stale cache is returned when origin is unreachable, preventing cascading failures.

30-60s
BGP convergence for intra-AS failover
5-10x
Traffic absorption via CDN during failover
24h
Max DNS TTL for SOA records

sections.title: 14. SRE Tooling & Infrastructure as Code
sections.description:
IaC · CONFIG MANAGEMENT · SRE TOOLING · TERRAFORM

Staff+ expectation: SRE teams manage infrastructure at scale through code, not SSH. Configuration drift is the enemy. At Staff+, you design the infrastructure-as-code strategy, choose idempotent tooling, and manage secrets without exposing them.

Infrastructure as Code (IaC): Declarative (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi) vs imperative (Ansible, Chef). Declarative is preferred at FAANG because it prevents drift: the system converges to the declared state. Terraform: plan -> apply cycle. State management with locking (DynamoDB, etcd).

Configuration Management: Tools: Chef (Meta), Puppet (Google historically), Ansible (widely used), internal systems (Google’s Borgcfg reads from Git). Key principle: configs are files in a repo, not manual changes. Changes go through code review.

Secret Management: HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, internal FAANG systems. Secrets never in config files. Delivered via: sidecar (Vault agent), filesystem, environment variables (least preferred as they leak). Rotation: DB passwords every 90d, TLS certs every 30-90d, API keys on rotation event.

TerraformPulumiAnsibleChefPacker
ToolTypeStateIdempotentLearning curve
Declarative IaCYes, remoteYesMedium
Declarative IaCYesYesMedium (uses code)
Config mgmtNoYesLow
Config mgmtNoYesHigh
Image builderNoYesLow

Staff+ design pattern: GitOps (Weave Flux, ArgoCD): the Git repo is the source of truth. A controller syncs the actual state to the repo state. Drift is detected and auto-remediated. At Meta: configs in Git, verified by CI, deployed by automation. At Google: Borg cfg in Git with multi-stage review.

sections.title: 15. Cost-Aware SRE: Efficiency, Right-Sizing & Waste Reduction
sections.description:
COST · EFFICIENCY · RIGHT-SIZING · FINOPS

Staff+ expectation: At FAANG scale, a 10% efficiency gain saves $100M+/year. Staff+ SREs own infrastructure cost, identify waste, and optimize resource usage without sacrificing reliability.

Cost Drivers at FAANG: Compute (40-60% of infra cost), Storage (15-25%), Networking (10-20%), Support (monitoring, logging, CI/CD) (5-10%). At Meta: AI training clusters consume 60%+ of all compute cycles. At Google: Borg achieves 50%+ utilization via overcommitment and preemption.

Efficiency Strategies:

  • Right-sizing: 70% of cloud compute is over-provisioned (Flexera 2024). Right-size based on peak 95th percentile, not max. Use auto-scaling to match demand.
  • Resource optimization: CPU: resize instance types (e.g., c5 vs m5 vs r5). Memory: reduce heap, tune GC (Go GC: set GOGC=100 vs 200 trades CPU for memory). Storage: move cold data to cheaper tiers (S3 Standard -> Glacier, from $0.023/GB to $0.004/GB).
  • Reserved/spot instances: 60%+ discount for 1-3 year commitments. Spot: 60-90% discount but can be terminated. Use spot for fault-tolerant batch workloads.
  • Waste elimination: Idle resources, orphaned volumes, oversized instances. At Google: automated resource reclamation returns idle resources to the pool within 24h.
10%
Efficiency gain = $100M+/yr at FAANG
70%
Cloud compute over-provisioned
60%+
AWS reserved instance discount (3yr)

sections.title: 16. Staff+ SRE Interview Strategy
sections.description:
INTERVIEW · STRATEGY · BEHAVIORAL · SYSTEM DESIGN

Staff+ expectation: SRE interviews at FAANG test different skills than SWE. You’ll be asked about system design for reliability, incident response thought process, and SRE-specific behavioral questions.

Company-Specific Emphasis:

  • Google: SRE interview loop: System Design (design for reliability), Coding (algorithms), Troubleshooting (hypothesis-driven debugging), Behavioral (leadership, incident command). Expect deep SLO/error budget questions.
  • Amazon: Bar raiser style. Expect "tell me about a time you fixed a production issue" with follow-ups on decisions made. Principal Engineers at Amazon are expected to design for failure (everything fails, all the time).
  • Meta: Focus on scale and debugging. "A service is slow, how do you find the bottleneck?" Distributed tracing, profiling, and systematic approach expected.
  • Netflix: DevOps culture. Every developer is on-call. Expect chaos engineering questions. "How do you build a system that survives an AWS region failure?"

Staff+ Behavioral Questions: "Tell me about the hardest production incident you resolved." Use STAR format: Situation (severity, impact), Task (your role), Action (specific actions with reasoning), Result (MTTR, follow-ups, systemic fixes). Focus on: coordination across teams, automated detection, and postmortem follow-through.

System Design for Reliability:  Common Staff+ questions: "Design a highly available payment system", "Design an incident management platform", "Design a monitoring system for 1M hosts", "How would you migrate a monolith to microservices with zero downtime?" For each, discuss: SLOs, failure modes, graceful degradation, canary strategy, rollback plan, observability, and error budget policy.

The Staff+ Difference: Your answers must show you’ve learned from real failures. Don’t just describe a system — describe what broke, what surprised you, and how you improved the system afterward. Google and Amazon both say: "We expect Staff+ candidates to talk about what went wrong, not just what went right."
sections.codeBlock:
# Incident simulation for interview practice
class IncidentSim:
    def __init__(self, scenario):
        self.scenario = scenario
        self.observations = []
        self.time = 0
        self.actions = []

    def observe(self, metric):
        self.observations.append((self.time, metric))
        return metric

    def act(self, description):
        self.actions.append((self.time, description))
        print(f"[T+{self.time}s] {description}")

    def elapse(self, seconds):
        self.time += seconds

# Interview fluency: micro-essay on reliability
# Pattern: "Design for failure means ..."
# Framing: "At Google scale, we assume ..."
# Decision: "I chose X over Y because ..."
# Verification: "After the change, error rate dropped from Z% to W%"

def reliability_frameworks():
    return {
        "design_for_failure": "Everything fails: network partitions, CPU throttling, memory corruption, clock skew, disk failure, process crashes. Design each component assuming all of its dependencies are down.",
        "defense_in_depth": "No single layer is responsible for reliability. Load balancer health checks, app-level timeouts, circuit breakers, retry budgets, and alerting all contribute.",
        "error_budget_decisions": "99.9% SLO means we accept 43 min of downtime/month. That budget is for deploys, experiments, and maintenance. When budget is exhausted, we stop.",
        "blameless_culture": "Postmortems ask what the system did wrong, not what the person did wrong. The person followed the best available understanding at the time.",
    }

TopicResourceWhy for Staff+
Site Reliability Engineering (Beyer, Jones, Petoff, Murphy)The SRE bible. Written by Google SREs.
The Site Reliability Workbook (Google)Practical exercises for SLOs, on-call, postmortems
Implementing Service Level Objectives (Hidalgo)Deep dive on SLO math, burn rates, alerting
The Art of Monitoring (Turnbull)Practical monitoring design patterns
Distributed Systems Observability (Cindy Sridharan)Understanding traces, logs, and metrics in depth
The Capacity Planning (Gunther)Queueing theory for capacity modeling
Chaos Engineering (Rosenthal, Jones)Principles and practice of chaos experiments
Blameless Postmortems (Slim.AI)Writing effective postmortems that drive change
Seeking SRE (Google)SRE culture, team topology, org design
Incident Management for Operators (Leonard)Practical incident command and coordination
SRE at Meta/Facebook (engineering.fb.com)Scaling reliability at social-network scale
Netflix Tech Blog / Chaos EngineeringSRE practices at streaming scale
